articles.title: Excessive nil pointer checks in Go
articles.summary: Go developers are advised to be cautious when using the nil pointer check, as excessive usage can lead to performance issues and potential crashes. The issue arises from the fact that Go's type system does not guarantee the absence of a value at runtime, making it necessary for developers to explicitly check for nil before accessing its fields or methods. This can result in unnecessary allocations and garbage collection, especially when working with large datasets. As such, developers should carefully consider their use of nil pointer checks to optimize performance and memory usage.

articles.title: Streaming services' obnoxiously loud ads become illegal on July 1 in California
articles.summary: Streaming platforms will be required to play ads louder than the content being watched in California starting July 1, as part of a law that prohibits any video streaming service from transmitting audio of commercial advertisements at a level higher than the accompanying video. The Commercial Advertisement Loudness Mitigation (CALM) Act, signed by California Governor Gavin Newsom in October 2025, requires streaming services to apply volume adjustments to US streams outside of California. Streaming providers will need to integrate loudness control into their server-side commercial insertion workflow and file-based processing to comply with the law.

articles.title: Why does kinetic energy increase quadratically, not linearly, with speed? (2011)
articles.summary: Kinetic energy increases quadratically with speed due to a phenomenon known as the work-energy theorem, which states that the net work done on an object is equal to its change in kinetic energy. This means that as an object moves faster, it requires more work to accelerate it further, resulting in increased kinetic energy.

articles.title: Making devenv start fast, and the whole nixpkgs with it - devenv
articles.summary: devenv auto activation runs devenv hook-should-activate on every shell prompt to decide whether you've stepped into a project directory and does almost nothing, its runtime is pure startup overhead, and it runs on every single prompt redraw, making it a significant performance issue. The dynamic loader's search for shared libraries can be slow due to the way Nix stores packages across the store, with most of this time spent knocking on 486 wrong doors before finding the right ones. A caching layer or deleting the dynamic loader altogether could significantly improve startup times, but neither approach has been implemented in nixpkgs yet.
